text: We hereby present the case of a seventy eight year old man who was investigated for rectal bleeding. A colonoscopy was performed under sedation but during the intubation of the sigmoid the endoscopist was immediately aware that a perforation had occurred and the patient was referred to the emergency surgical team. At the initial exploration, the sigmoid was stuck to the lateral abdominal wall and contain multiple diverticula but no evident perforation. There was blood in the pelvis. An almost complete rectal lesion was identified. The two stumps appeared well supplied and clean, with regular margins.

Traditionally, we would have treated this patient with a sigmoid resection and proximal end colostomy, but in this case, due to the good conditions of the stumps and the lack of gross contamination, we opted for a laparoscopic immediate anastomosis with diverting loop ileostomy. The two stumps were joined with a vicral double-layer manual anastomosis. In our opinion, this approach should be considered the gold standard also in large colonic perforations. In fact, it is well known as a high percentage of patients having a Hartman's resection are never reversed due to the risks involved, whereas reversing a loop ileostomy is a much easier and safer operation. Clearly, an immediate anastomosis requires optimal local conditions with no gross contamination and margins neat and well supplied, and the availability of advanced laparoscopic expertise.

After abundant pelric washout, the operation was completed with a pelric drain and a diverting loop ileostomy. The postoperative recovery was uneventful and the patient was discharged on day five in good general and local conditions. A water-soluble contrast enema performed after three months revealed a patent anastomosis with no leak. The reversal of the ileostomy was therefore planned.
